speaker: interviewer
text: Barrack with the Hungarian women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to our camp, we were in Mannerlager, I-- I think it was, E or D. Next to us was the camp from the Hungarian women. And we had tried to send to B Lager, to our parents, notes back that we are here. This was around the 7th or 8th of July. As a matter of fact, one of the women-- her name was Agnes Ganzfried, from Nyiregyhaza. Why I remember Nyiregyhaza-- it was a town-- before my mother got married, my mother was working in Nyiregyhaza for a rabbi for 16 years. That's the reason that I remember Nyiregyhaza, in Hungary. And her name was Agnes Ganffried. And I gave her notes to throw it over to my mother and father, because we wanted them to know where we are. And she saw us children-- it was very seldom to see in Birkenau, in-- in-- in the camp, children. She threw me a piece of bread. And we were so hungry, a piece of bread-- it meant so much for me. I never will forget her name, and I always-- when I see an Hungarian person, I always ask about her. I never know if my parents-- or Walter sent notes to his parents-- if they received it. We don't know. We know only that, on 10th or 11th of July, they rounded up B Lager, the SS, with the Sonderkommando. The Sonderkommando was the-- the kommando that worked for the German in the-- in the crematoriums. And then you had, in the camps, kapos. The kapos were in the camps. The Sonderkommando worked actually only in the crematorium, to-- to do the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were inmates who worked-- the Sonderkommandos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inmates, yeah. The Sonderkommando. Anyway, they let them live only for three, four months, and then they put them in the gas chambers. But they had everything. They had food. They had the best-- because people came-- they had everything. They had Hungarian salami, they had food, they had everything. But on 10th or 11th of July, they rounded up B Lager. And B Lager was not so easy for them, because people had-- they smelled something that is going to happen. They were already months in-- in Birkenau. And they didn't want to go-- I heard it later on from the-- from the kommandos, they didn't want to go willingly. They brought in the SS with the guns, with the kapos with the stick, and throw them on the trucks. They throw them on the trucks and transfer them straight to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on 10th and 11th of July, that they took my mom and my dad. [SOBBING] I-- I-- I never cried! I never cried! [SOBBING] And then they took them on 10th and 11th of July and threw them in the gas chambers. [SOBBING] Such good parents! [SOBBING] I live with it every day. I cannot forget it. [SOBBING] Why did they do that? [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew it, on 10th and 11th of July, they took them and they threw them in the gas chambers, the whole camp-- women one day and men, with my brother, everybody that was there. And we didn't cry, Walter and me. We didn't even cry. We couldn't cry. We knew already what happened, and it will happen to us, too. It was the 10th and 11th of July, 1944, that they put all our families in the crematorium. And we knew about it. They told us. They-- they go in, and they close the doors in the crematorium. And then the-- the-- the SS looks in the hole to see how-- and the strong people crawled on top of the other ones to the top, when they put the gas in. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, after they-- they gassed, they opened the doors. And then the Sonderkommando put them in the ovens to burn them. Suddenly, we became-- we became, in-- in-- in Mannerlager, adults, Walter and me. I had-- we had to start working. I worked with Walter, I think, in the-- the Rollwagenkommando. 10 or 12 children-- I don't remember-- we had to take a wagon from horses. And-- and as horses, we children had to pull the wagon. We went out, first off, in the morning, the appels, and then with the wagon we had to go out and then load it with clothing or woods for the crematorium. And next to us, the kapos were going with-- with the sticks and hitting us. And we had-- we had to push the wheel so it rolled. It was so hard-- children-- hungry. On the-- on the road, on the side, was-- was growing green raspberries, and we-- we took them. We ate green raspberries. It was so hard. Every time out, and then standing appels. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- in the Rollwagen, I don't know how long I worked-- Walter and me. And all the other children that were with us were-- most of them were Czechs, from Czechoslovakia. And Walter and me, we were the only ones from Austria. And they-- they were a little bit separated. Even we were Jewish, but they called us the [NON-ENGLISH]. And one of the guys that came from the ghetto and was with us in-- in Familienlager-- certain names I remember, and this was Helmut Schpreitzer. And he became, in Mannerlager, a laufer. And a laufer was a-- a-- a runner for the SS. He was in the front of the camp. In front of the camp was always a building. When you walked out or in, the SS was there and counting you-- by-- by the gate. By every one was a-- a little house with SS. He was the laufer. He was the runner for the SS that was to give orders in the camp. So they used him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a little guy, Helmut, and he had boots on. Pants that-- you know, like when you-- on horses? He was very nice and neat dressed. He knew German. I remember him from the camp because when he came by, by our block, he gave me a little piece of bread. And this was God-- God, was this.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp-- in the-- in-- in the-- in this block where we were, the Strafblock, we saw a lot of things. They had a place where they hit you. When they catched you doing something wrong or something, there was a bench you had to go in, with the feet in, and you had to bend down. And the other side was SS, pulling your hand. And on both sides were standing an SS and hitting you, or you got 25, or you got 50. And if they catch somebody, they brought him in our barrack and they gave him 25 or 50. After they gave it to him, he was laying-- he was pieces of meat. There was nothing left of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we saw it. During the time that we were there, there were people that would run away and they catch them. So they got 50, and then they hanged them. We had to go and see-- all this we saw. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but interesting is that when I-- when we were there already a certain time-- and we never cried. We never cried, because we know-- we knew that we end up in this crematorium. We knew that, that there is no way out of here. We lived in this Birkenau. We were-- we thought that we are alone in the world. There is nothing about us. Nobody knows about us. Nobody cares about us. They-- we are here only to-- to-- to go in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked in the Rollwagenkommando. It was terrible. It was very hard. Children as horses. And we-- we-- we brought-- all around the crematorium was lumber, cutted in pieces-- I don't remember-- five or six foot. We had to bring lumber there. We took clothes-- for clothes from people that brought, to clean. All kind of work we had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one of these days, when I worked in the Rollwagenkommando, they came back-- I don't know what happened. They decided to take the temperature from people. And they took my temperature, and they found out that I have a stomach typhus. I am sick. When they found out I had typhus-- this is a sickness that, you know, you can spread it, probably. I don't know. They put me in the Krankenblock, what is for sick people. So I-- I left the Rollwagenkommando. And in the moment I went in, in this Krankenblock, I was really finished. I had high temperature. I start, fantasies. I saw German all around my house. Terrible fantasies. I was on the third floor. But how sick I was, I knew if I stayed there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, a third floor? On the third bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third bunk. the-- the beds. There was no three-floor barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three bunks

speaker: interviewee
text: Three bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on the third bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the head right to the front. And next to me was laying-- there's three, four names I never forget. A guy from French. His name was Fred Wolfson. He was a little bit older than me. And when he saw me, as a kid, and I couldn't eat-- I did nothing-- he pushed in my mouth a little bit  of food, always. When-- when we got the soup or whatever, he forced it in in my mouth. I knew when I am staying there, I screamed, I want to go back to work. I screamed, I want to go back to work. And in this Krankenblock, on the corner was a little room. And I never forget when-- when they catched a man talking to a woman, through the-- through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fences or something-- or he worked on a-- with a wagon, and he went in another block-- in another-- another lager, B Lager or A Lager. When they catched him, they took him in this room and castrated. I don't know, in English, what it means. They cut off--

speaker: interviewer
text: Castrated, castrating.

speaker: interviewee
text: They castrated him alive. No-- and I remember when I was laying there, sick and fantasy, he came out-- and even in the Krankenlager, in the middle was the brick come in and on the sides, the bed. So he walked like this-- his feet open and screaming. And when they did the operation-- what operation-- probably with a knife-- I don't know what. But when they did it, there was such a screaming, the whole block was shivering, the whole block. I know-- I-- I thought-- I-- I-- I knew that this is my end if I stayed there, and I start screaming, I want to go back to work. I want to go back to work. And this Fred Wolfson always gave me a little bit food. And suddenly, they said, you go back to work. I left, and I went back to the Mannerlager. And the next day after I went out, the next day, they took the whole Krankenblock, everybody that was there, put them in the-- in the gas chambers. Nobody left. If I would be there one more day, I would be ashes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back there. Then my work was start cleaning the camp. And we had a-- a-- a couple-- a Polack, from Poland. He was in the camp of criminal charges. He had-- everybody had-- he had to wear, like, a little label.

speaker: interviewer
text: The triangle.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was different things. One was gay, one was criminal, one was communist, Jews. Everybody had his label. He was there, and he was so mean. He had-- his arm was here cut. I don't know why-- from before the war. I don't know what. But when we were bending down and cleaning-- in-- in the camp, everything was little stones. They had to keep it stones, and-- and grass-- I don't know. In winter it was muddy, everything. But everything we had to clean. Always he hit, in between the eyes. Always he hit us. It was terrible. It was terrible. Walter-- Walter, as far as I know, he worked in the Kleiderkammer. He worked where-- where, when the people arrived in transports, they took all the suitcases, all the clothes, and there were special places they had to separate everything. Walter worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name for that barrack? Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they called it Kleiderkammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canada, yes. Yes, it was called Canada. He worked in Canada. During the time-- Walter, when he worked in Canada, he start bringing us clothing-- underwear, certain things that nobody could see. I-- I remember we had a jacket that, in the back, was cut out. It was stripes in it-- the grey and blue, so we had a jacket. During our stay, the transport cames day and night, day and night. I remember they came from Lodz-- ghetto Lodz. And they told us the stories, the Sonderkommando. That when they came from Lodz, they took the rabbi from Lodz with his wife and children, and they didn't put him in the gas chamber. They put him in the crematorium. And they threw, first of all, his children-- first of all, they took all his hair out. Then they threw every child in the crematorium, one by one. Then they took his wife, and then they took him. The Sonderkommando told us-- and we came very close to the crematorium with the-- with the lumber, too-- before people went into the gas chambers, too, the SS-- they took babies and threw them in the air and were shooting at them. They took babies and threw them on the wall. They took babies and tear them apart. It's-- it's-- it's-- it's not-- it's impossible to think how they could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Sonderkommando told you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, certain things we saw, too, when we came to the crematorium, because they didn't care. There were not afraid of us. We saw-- we were-- on the end, we saw our camp-- one side was right where the railroads came, where the people arrived. And some transports, they made selection. Some transport, the whole transport, right as it came, they put them straight-- they went to the gas chamber. Not one left over. I remember when the Hungarians came. July, August, there came transports from Hungary. I remember them good because, even when they selected the people-- they were next to us whole-- A, B, C, D, E, F-- different camps where they put in the Hungarian men. And they called them-- we called them margarina because they came straight from Budapest, or from the country. They were-- they were nourished. They had good food. And suddenly they came in in the camp, they right away became sick. Many died of them. But they couldn't stand it. They fell. They couldn't-- they couldn't get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are-- we are-- we are already-- all the people in the camp, we called them margarina because they couldn't stand it. They couldn't get it. Margarina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margarina, called margarine, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soft, like margarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Substitute butter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember, in October there was a stand up by the Sonderkommando. First of all, every three months they changed the Sonderkommando. But they-- it was in October. But before that, I-- I-- I-- what I want to mention, what was very important-- in July or August, even in May, when we were with the families together, when we came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had air raids. And we saw thousands of airplanes coming. American, British airplanes came over the camp, and the SS went in the bunkers. And we inmates stood up and looked at them and prayed. Maybe they will bomb a crematorium. Maybe they will bomb us, at least, so we should die because it was so terrible. But they flew over us. And all around Auschwitz there were factories. Buna, Blechhammer-- all kind of different camps where they made-- weapon factories, munition factories. So they came to bomb these places. But they didn't have four bombs, five bombs to-- to bomb the-- the crematorium, or at least one bomb to bomb the railroads. I mean, all this time, there was never, never help from the outside. That somebody would bomb the railroads bringing people from all over to-- to be gassed. Nobody did nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Partisans or whatever-- where they have been? Where are the Polacks? Where are all the Polish people that said they will help us-- they helped us? Nobody was there. Not one day I can remember that somebody helped us. And when we saw the airplane, I says, maybe something will happen but nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: In October, the Sonderkommando bombed-- we saw it from our barracks. We stood outside. We saw smoke. We heard the noise. And we were told that they bombed our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sonderkommando-- bombed one crematorium. We had to stay appel, day and night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even when-- if somebody ran away, they made appels. We had to stay in the cold, in the freezing cold. We had to stay 12, 14 hours, till they counted everyone to be sure. Dead had to be counted, too. Live or dead you had-- the numbers had to be OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't have to mention we were hungry. We got, once a day, a little bit black soup, a little piece of margarine, and a little piece of bread. The only thing in our mind-- we knew that we will be gassed-- how to find a piece of bread? How to find something to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were always going, looking. I don't know. We didn't have any more mind. I mean, a month ago, they put our parents in the gas chambers, and we didn't cry. We never could cry. We never could do nothing. We were helpless. The whole world is against us, and we are going to gas chambers, too. But when we saw the Sonderkommando did it, we were very happy. We was so happy. But after two or three days-- probably Sonderkommando, they were working 500, 600, or 700 people. They killed a couple SS, but they got everybody. Nobody-- the whole history from Auschwitz, as far as I know, one or two people escaped. Nobody could escape from there. And that means that all the surrounding, from Birkenau to Auschwitz-- that I, all my life thought we were on an island, that I later found out there's all kinds of cities and towns and villages the trains go to. Nobody was there to help. Nobody did nothing. Nobody could escape. Except, the whole history from Birkenau, maybe one or two escaped. But when they got him, and-- we had to stand, till they got him, on appel. If you couldn't stay-- always with a stick. Everything was with a stick. We were called, already, Musselman in-- in-- in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Musselmann was-- was people that they were ready for the crematorium. It was-- you were only bones, actually. You had nothing on yourself. But we tried-- how-- how-- how we were not strong. When we were tired and we couldn't stand, we knew if we would go down, the next day we're in the crematorium. And this kept us going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a Musselmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- we all were Musselmann. We really didn't have nothing to eat. We were hungry. We were tired. We-- it-- it was something that you knew that, if you don't do it, they hit you. And sometimes they hit you-- every day, you had dead people. We had-- with the wagons, people went every morning and collected the dead. After the appellplatz and they counted everyone, they took them on wagons and-- and took them to the crematoriums. And time went by in-- in Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were all the time in the same block-- in the Strafblock. We saw all the punishments that they gave to the people. That they cut them in pieces. I got, once, tanned-- tanned on my behind, too. And it-- it was terrible. It is terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't clean the right thing. Even he hit me with his hand. But I got punished. I got 10. And the pain, it was terrible. The pain was terrible. Because they hit-- you don't have any more meat even. They hit the bones. It-- it takes weeks till you can really stand on your legs. But the-- the other guys that they catch, and they got 25, 50. They were laying there like pieces of meat. They couldn't get up. And it was enjoyment for them-- the-- the SS, with the boots, dressed nice, strong. And they had sticks like this. And it was, for them, an enjoyment to see that. There were many things in the crematorium, too. There came transport-- one transport came, and there was a beautiful woman with a child. And before they went into the gas chambers, there was a square where they had to stand. And she took the revolver from the SS, because he-- he-- he was shooting-- he tried to shoot the-- to take her child away or something. So she took the gun, and, so another SS right away shot her. There were many things-- they were enjoying to-- to play with children-- to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I saw-- this accident, I didn't see it. The Sonderkommando told me. But I saw-- when we were-- I saw-- I heard the screaming from children, when they threw them in the air. I heard shooting after them. When I went into the crematorium, I saw pieces of meat when they threw the children on the-- on the wall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they enjoyed it. Because the Sonderkommando told us they-- they always when they put the people in the gas chambers, they had glass windows they looked through till-- it took probably five to 10 minutes till the last one died. Some people, even, they-- they put in the crematorium alive, when they were-- had a strong heart and they could live it over, they threw them-- threw them in the crematorium alive. But there's a lot of things that we were told, before-- there were people that were years in Birkenau and Auschwitz. That before the crematoriums, they had-- they had, like, walls, and they threw chlorine on the people, different deaths. They were shooting people. But there was times when all the five crematoriums were full speed working and still-- still people came. And after the crematoriums they had a-- a soccer place-- a-- a size of a soccer place or football place-- that was digged out-- was a big ditch. And on the end of the ditch they had a little-- they had a gas chamber, too. And when the crematoriums were working full, they-- they sent people to the gas chambers and didn't even burn them. They threw them right in the-- in the ditch by the thousands. And when it was full, probably, they covered it. I don't know. I know after the war I went there, and I was digging there. And you can find the bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Birkenau till end of December. When they were talking about the Russians came close to Birkenau-- it was end of December-- they sended us on a march. We had to get all together, in rows, and they call it, like, the death march. We left, I think, around by 60,000 to 65,000 people, we left Birkenau when the Russians came. We start marching. It was December. I remember it was snow. And we start marching to Gleiwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the sides, SS with machine guns. We start walking. They didn't give us special food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Walter and me-- because he worked in the clothing chamber-- we had-- shoes, we had. And we had a jacket, like maybe a coat. But in the back was the mark from a concentration camp-- was cut out, with blue and grey marks in it. And we are marched to Gleiwitz, and I don't know how many days it took. But from Gleiwitz we went to another place, and we went in a forest.
